free_text: A study of the 16O(d,p)17O reaction.
Spread in beam energy was 10 keV.
Counter telescope located on a movable
arm which was rotated from outside the chamber through
a high vacuum seal. The scattering angle could be set
by remote control to withing +-0.2 degr.Telescope
consisted of two surface barrier detectors, one behind
the other. The front counter was a fully depleted
delta-E/delta-x counter 0.8 microns thick. The second
counter had an 0.2 m depletion layer and recorded the
residal energy  E - Delta-E of the detected particle.
Faraday cup and integrator.
Pulse multiplier was used for particle identification.
Counting rate was kept below 5000 per sec to maintain
good resolution and minimise background.
Target system was mounted on the lid of the chamber.
Three targets could be placed in the chamber
simultaneously. The targets were either WO3 backed by 5
mu-g/cm2 thickness carbon or self supporting SiO2
foils. All were less than 100 mu-g/cm2 thick. The
target position was adjusted to minimise the energy
spread and was 6 keV thick for 12 MeV deuterons.
Elastically scattered particles from a gold foil placed
between the scattering chamber and the Faraday cup were
examined at 30 degrees.
Digitizing error
WO3 target was used except at angles forward of 40
degrees where the large W(d,d) peak starts to obscure
the 16O(d,d) peak. A SiO2 target enabled measurements
to be taken in to 17.5 degrees.
Digitizing error
Error from the graphic bar
The statistical errors were kept below 1%. This error
together with the 0.2 degree uncertainty in the
scattering angle and an extraction error at the very
forward angles gave a total error of 5% at 17.5 degrees
decreasing to less than 2% for angles grater than 80
degrees.
Fig.5
WO3 target was used.
A background correction was made.
Digitizing error
Error from the graphic bar
Maximum statistical error was 10% decreasing to below
2% beyond 60 degrees.
The errors involved in the background and extraction
were different for each point and generally larger than
the statistical errors.
Fig.6
WO3 target 50% enriched was used. The presence of small
amounts of O18 in the targets led to an uncertainty.
At angles forward of 110 degrees the 16O(p,p) and
17O(p,p)peaks overlapped and an extraction was
necessary.
Digitizing error
Error from the graphic bar
A 5% error was placed separately on each point.
Fig.7
WO3 target was used except at angles forward of 40
degrees where the large W(d,d) peak starts to obscure
the 16O(d,d) peak. A SiO2 target enabled measurements
to be taken in to 17.5 degrees.
